GST Calculator Tax

How to Calculate GST on Freight and Transportation Charges

Confused about GST on shipping? Learn when to charge 5%, 12%, or 18% on freight and how to calculate it correctly.

18 June 2026

When shipping goods to a customer, adding freight charges to your invoice is standard practice. However, determining the correct GST rate to apply to those transportation charges can be incredibly confusing. Do you charge 5%? 18? Or the rate of the product inside the box?

Let’s clarify the rules and show you how to use a GST Calculator to get the math right every time.

Rule 1: Composite Supply (Freight included in the Product Invoice)

If you are a seller dispatching goods to a buyer and you include the freight/delivery charges directly on the same invoice, this is treated as a Composite Supply.

Under GST law, in a composite supply, the principal supply (the goods being sold) determines the tax rate for all ancillary services (the freight).

Example:

You sell a mobile phone (18% GST) for ₹10,000 and charge ₹500 for delivery. Because the mobile phone is the principal supply, the delivery charge must also be taxed at 18%.

  • Use the GST Calculator.
  • Base Value = ₹10,500 (10,000 + 500)
  • Rate = 18%
  • GST Amount = ₹1,890.

If you sold a book (Exempt - 0% GST) and charged ₹100 for delivery, the delivery charge is also taxed at 0%.

Rule 2: GTA Services (Goods Transport Agency)

If you are hiring a dedicated Goods Transport Agency (GTA) to move your goods, the GTA has two options for charging GST:

  1. 5% GST (No ITC): The GTA does not claim Input Tax Credit on their trucks/fuel and charges 5%. Under this option, the recipient of the service (you) usually has to pay the tax under the Reverse Charge Mechanism (RCM).
  2. 12% GST (With ITC): The GTA claims ITC and charges 12% on the invoice under the forward charge mechanism.

You can verify the transport agency’s active status and classification using a GSTIN Validator.

Calculating Reverse Charge (RCM) on Freight

If your GTA opts for the 5% RCM route, they will give you a bill without GST. You must calculate the 5% yourself, pay it directly to the government via your GSTR-3B, and then claim it back as ITC.

  • Invoice from GTA: ₹5,000.
  • Enter 5000 in the GST Calculator and select 5%.
  • You must deposit ₹250 to the government.

Always ensure the transportation service uses the correct SAC code (e.g., 9965 or 9967). You can search for exact transport-related codes using our HSN Code Lookup. Accurate calculation and reporting ensure you meet your GST Due Dates without audit complications.

TE
